heading: MODULO UNDICESIMO
card_summary: Modulo Undicesimo of the third Canticle catalogues the "instruments" of heavenly harmony: the celestial globe, the human tongue, the resurrected human body, the angelic tongue, the angelic and blessed minds, and the Holy Spirit as the animating breath. Zorzi draws on Plato's sirens, Avicenna's anatomy, Kabbalistic gematria linking the 613 Mosaic commandments to the divine names Elohim and Adonai, Augustine on David's psalms, Boethius on bodily harmony, and Zoroaster on human self-perception of harmony. A sustained digression argues that Hebrew is the original and eschatological universal language, preserved through Eber and Abraham, and destined to be restored after the final judgment of fire prophesied in Zephaniah. This summary covers the opening portion of this long section.
essay:
# MODULO UNDICESIMO: The Instruments of Heavenly Harmony

*Note: This summary covers the opening portion of this long section (pp. 2625–ca. 2474 of the Italian text), as the chunk runs to 8455 words and the analysis is based on the first ca. 6000 words.*

## Central Argument

Modulo Undicesimo enumerates and anatomizes the six principal "instruments" (*strumenti*/*instrumenta*) through which the heavenly concert of praise is performed. The section proceeds as a structured musical-theological treatise: each instrument is described in terms of its physical or spiritual nature, its eschatological perfection, its scriptural warrants, and its place in the great harmonic order that Zorzi has been constructing throughout the *De Harmonia Mundi*. The organizing metaphor is that of a divine theatre or concert hall — a cosmos built to perfect resonance — in which every voice, body, and mind is an instrument tuned by God.

## Instrument I: The Celestial Globe (pp. 2625–2458)

Zorzi opens by recapitulating the celestial music of the spheres, invoking Plato's sirens (*Repubblica* X) who sit upon the orbits and produce their melodies. The presiding musician is God himself, to whom Orpheus addressed the line: *"Tu accordi il cielo come una cetra sonora"* ("You tune the heaven like a resounding lyre"). The architecture of heaven is compared favourably to the theatrical spaces (*theatri e scene*) of antiquity, which were built with acoustic chambers and proportional vases to amplify actors' voices. Zorzi insists that art can only imitate nature, never surpass it, and that the divine throne-room surpasses every human construction in harmonic perfection. Augustine (note 658) is cited on David's role as the supreme psalmist, who expressed the unity of the heavenly city through "diversi suoni razionali e melodie temperate."

## Instrument II: The Human Tongue (pp. 2458–2463)

Zorzi turns to the physiology of vocal speech, citing Avicenna's account of the organs of phonation: fauces, palate, teeth, lips, throat, lungs, trachea, and pectoral muscles. The tongue, even in its corporeal form, will exercise its function in heavenly praise, as attested by Psalm 149 ("Le lodi divine sulle loro bocche"). Crucially, Zorzi argues for a doctrine of eschatological bodily perfection: all deformed, distorted, or speech-impaired bodies will be restored to perfect consonance at the resurrection, so that no defect of voice remains. The medium of sound in the supracelestial region is then examined: following Moses and Psalm 148 ("Le acque che sono al di sopra del cielo lodino il nome del Signore"), Zorzi argues that waters exist above the heavens; the ninth sphere (primum mobile or crystalline heaven) is aqueous; the empyrean above it is aereal with a certain solidity (*soliditas*), suitable for the thrones of the blessed and for the propagation of sound.

> *"...il cielo superiore, dove si trovano le sedi dei beati è di natura aerea, seppure dotato di una certa stabilità, in modo che i seggi dei beati e il trono del Principe siano saldi, come si addice a quanti vi siedono e a tutti gli ospiti già glorificati."*

<cite>p. 2460 (Italian translation)</cite>

### Hebrew as the Eschatological Language

A major digression (pp. 2460–2463) argues that the language of the blessed will be Hebrew. Zorzi's reasoning is cumulative: (1) the Law was revealed, the Gospel preached by Christ, and all divine hymns composed in Hebrew (the Apocalypse alone was given in Greek); (2) Hebrew is "*mysteriosissima lingua*" in its letter-forms, vowel points, accents, and numerical values, enabling Kabbalistic operations of letter-permutation (*revolutio litterarum*) and gematria (*supputatio numerorum*); (3) the Hebrew letters themselves are said to have been inscribed on the throne of God, and Moses formed earthly script on their heavenly model; (4) Hebrew was the primordial language, existing before the Babylonian confusion of tongues, which Augustine interpreted as a divine punishment for the arrogance of Nimrod; (5) Hebrew is therefore fittingly the final language, to be restored after the eschatological fire.

> *"...si dice che quelle stesse lettere sono incise sul trono di Dio, che è il cielo; sul loro modello sono state formate le stesse lettere che Mosè impiegò per primo nel porre per iscritto la legge."*

<cite>p. 2461 (Italian translation)</cite>

Zorzi invokes the etymology of *'ivri* / "Hebrew" from the root *'avar* (to cross over, citing the Hebrew אֲבַר), the preservation of the language through Eber and the pious family that refused to participate in Nimrod's hubris, and Augustine's analysis of the tower of Babel as both technical over-reaching and linguistic fall. The eschatological restoration is grounded in Zephaniah 3:8–9: *"In igne zeli mei devorabitur omnis terra. Et tunc reddam populis meis labium electum, ut invocent nomen Domini, et serviant ei humero uno."*

## Instrument III: The Resurrected Human Body (pp. 2464–2467)

The human body is itself an instrument of harmony. Zorzi cites Augustine on the invisible proportionality of the body's interior, and Boethius: *"La vivacità della ragione nel corpo si mescola con un certo adattamento e si realizza in un efficace temperamento, come le voci gravi e quelle acute in un'unica armonia."* He also cites an aphorism attributed to Zoroaster (the Chaldean Oracles): *"L'uomo esulta per l'armonia che avverte in sé"* — perceiving harmony better than understanding it.

The most technically Kabbalistic passage follows: the 613 commandments of Mosaic law (248 positive precepts corresponding to the bones of the human body; 365 negative precepts corresponding to its nerves and to the days of the year) are linked to the divine names *Elohim* (אלהים) and *Adonai* (אדני) by means of the Kabbalistic technique of *milui* (letter-filling). The filled letters of *Elohim* yield 296, those of *Adonai* yield 316; their sum is 612; adding one for the unity that results from their conjunction gives 613. This mystery signifies that the fullness of divine judgment (*plenitudo iudicii*) under the name Elohim has been given to Jesus, citing John 5:22 ("Il Padre, infatti, non giudica nessuno... ma ha dato al Figlio ogni giudizio") and Matthew 12:36 (accountability for every idle word at the Last Judgment).

> *"...nella legge mosaica furono promulgati tanti precetti affermativi quante sono le ossa presenti nel corpo umano, ossia duecentoquarantotto, e tanti precetti negativi quanti sono i nervi di quello stesso corpo ovvero, non senza ragione, trecentosessantacinque..."*

<cite>p. 2465 (Italian translation)</cite>

The section concludes with Ezekiel 37 (the valley of dry bones) read as a harmony: bones joining to bones, then nerves and flesh returning, figure the eschatological reassembly of the body into perfect consonance. Augustine is cited on the resurrected body sounding praise in proportion to each person's consonance with God in observing the divine precepts.

## Instrument IV: The Angelic Tongue (pp. 2467–2471)

Paul's reference to "the tongues of men and of angels" (1 Cor. 13:1) establishes that angels possess their own language. Zorzi argues this angelic speech operates not by audible sound but by impressing concepts directly in the mind of the interlocutor — a communication that surpasses vocal speech. He draws on Plato and Aristotle on the tongue as the instrument of mental expression, and on Marcus Varro's etymology of *lingua* from *ligare* (to bind food). The analogy of proximity and volume is developed: one shouts to someone far away, whispers to someone near, but if one could unite with another's soul, no sound at all would be needed. This describes angelic and demonic communication alike.

A careful distinction follows between demonic deception (which works by transmitting phantasmic forms through the "animal" faculty, stirring dormant passions) and angelic illumination (which works by reflecting the immutable Truth, as Augustine explains). Angels consult the immutable Truth as an eternal law in their heavenly court, participating in the *veritas creatrix* to which they tend as to the Fountain of Life. They occasionally emit sensible sound, as at the Ascension ("Uomini di Galilea, perché ve ne state a guardare il cielo?"), and the archangel's trumpet will summon all to judgment.

## Instrument V: Angelic and Blessed Minds (pp. 2471–2473)

Since heavenly speech proceeds by mind rather than sound, the minds themselves are instruments resonating a harmony sweeter than any vocal concert. Zorzi grounds this in the phenomenology of human intellectual concord: when interlocutors find agreement of thought, a pleasure filled with sweetness arises — a foretaste of the beatific consonance. Augustine is cited for the proposition that beatitude itself consists in consonant adherence to God, while misery consists in dissonance from him. The Magnificat is introduced as the supreme instance of mind-and-spirit conjoined in praise.

> *"Questa è l'autentica armonia, ovvero quella della mente, alla quale si riferisce ogni melodia vocale che si realizza mediante strumenti naturali ovvero per mezzo di strumenti artificiali e frutto dell'ingegno, come al più autentico strumento di tutte le lodi."*

<cite>p. 2472 (Italian translation)</cite>

Augustine's commentary on Psalm 47 (*Psallite sapienter*) is quoted: "Inneggia con sapienza chi loda con l'illuminazione della mente" — a formulation central to Zorzi's elevation of intellectual over merely vocal praise.

## Instrument VI: The Holy Spirit as Animating Breath (opening, p. 2473–)

The sixth instrument, introduced at the end of the sampled text, is the Holy Spirit and the divine Word, without which none of the preceding instruments could sound. Zorzi cites Matthew 10:19–20 ("non vos loquimini, sed Spiritus sanctus est, qui loquitur in vobis") and identifies the Spirit as the source of the consonance between mind and God — grounding this in the Johannine identification of Christ as Truth itself ("Ego sum veritas"). The devil is characterized as the first rebel against this consonance, one who "ab initio in veritate non stetit."

## Sources and Technical Terms

- **Scripture**: Ps. 148/149, Ps. 47, Ps. 35, Ps. 50, Is. 29:13, Ezek. 37, Zeph. 3:8–9, Matt. 10:19–20, Matt. 12:36, John 5:22, 1 Cor. 13:1, Acts 1:11
- **Patristics**: Augustine (extensively — *De Civitate Dei*, Psalm commentaries), Jerome
- **Philosophy**: Plato (Republic X, sirens; Timaeus, tongue), Aristotle (*De anima*), Boethius (*De institutione musica*)
- **Science/medicine**: Avicenna (anatomy of phonation)
- **Philology**: Marcus Varro (*lingua* etymology)
- **Kabbalah**: *milui* (letter-filling technique), gematria of 613 commandments, divine names Elohim (אלהים) and Adonai (אדני), Hebrew letters inscribed on the divine throne
- **Chaldean Oracles**: "Zoroaster" on human self-perception of harmony
- **Key terms**: *concentus*, *consonantia*, *dissonantia*, *empyreum* (from Greek ἐμπύρ, fire), *milui* (Kabbalistic filling), *'avar* (עָבַר, to cross over, root of "Hebrew"), *lingua angelica*, *veritas immutabilis*
